skill.name: recommendation-letter-assistant
skill.description: Helps faculty and mentors draft standardized recommendation letters for medical trainees.
skill.summary: Generate structured recommendation letter drafts for medical trainees based on competency frameworks.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 42 static findings are false positives after evaluation. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts matched metadata field names (e.g., 'evaluated_on', 'evaluator_version') and ordinary documentation text (e.g., 'competencies'), not actual cryptographic code. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts matched markdown code block fences showing example bash commands inside SKILL.md documentation, not executable scripts. The 'path traversal sequence' alerts matched security checklist items documenting what to prevent (e.g., 'No unauthorized file system access (../)'), not actual traversal code. The 'system reconnaissance' alerts matched field names like 'scientific_integrity' and normal workflow documentation. The only legitimate risk is execution of the packaged Python script (scripts/main.py), which is a benign text generator with no network calls, no data exfiltration, and no file system modification.

content.value_statement: Drafting recommendation letters for medical trainees takes time and must follow structured guidelines. This skill generates consistent letter drafts based on applicant strengths and your relationship with them.
actual_capabilities: Generate letter openings matched to relationship type (mentor, course director, research PI)
Draft body paragraphs highlighting clinical competencies and personal strengths
Create peer comparison statements to rank the applicant among their cohort
Produce closing statements with adjustable enthusiasm level
Structure output in a standardized format with opening, body, and closing sections
limitations: Does not verify factual accuracy of the content it generates
Cannot customize letter format beyond the templates provided in the script
Does not integrate with application systems like ERAS or AMCAS
Requires you to provide accurate assessment of applicant strengths and context

use_cases.title: Faculty Mentor Writes Residency Recommendation
use_cases.description: A clinical faculty member drafts a residency recommendation letter for a medical student they supervised. The skill generates a letter with competency-based language and a strong closing endorsement.

use_cases.title: Research PI Recommends for Fellowship
use_cases.description: A research principal investigator writes a fellowship recommendation for a research assistant. The letter emphasizes research skills, analytical ability, and productivity in the lab.

use_cases.title: Course Director Creates Dean Letter
use_cases.description: A course director drafts a standardized evaluation letter for a medical school applicant. The output aligns with MSPE-style format and addresses core competencies expected by residency programs.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Medical Student Recommendation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a medical student named Sarah Chen. I was their clinical mentor for 6 months. Their strengths are clinical skills, work ethic, and teamwork. The context is residency application for internal medicine.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user needing a straightforward letter

prompt_templates.title: Competency-Focused Letter
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a recommendation letter for James Rodriguez. I was his research PI for 2 years. His key strengths are research, leadership, and communication. The context is a surgical residency application. Focus on specific examples of his analytical abilities.
prompt_templates.scenario: User who wants emphasis on measurable competencies

prompt_templates.title: Research Fellowship Recommendation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a letter for Lisa Patel. I supervised her in my lab for 3 years. Her strengths are research, professionalism, and communication. The context is a postdoctoral fellowship in neuroscience. Highlight her first-author publication and grant writing skills.
prompt_templates.scenario: Research supervisor seeking a detailed research-focused letter

prompt_templates.title: Dean Letter with Peer Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Dean letter style evaluation for Michael Kim. I was his course director for one academic year. His strengths are clinical skills, leadership, and professionalism. Context is orthopedic surgery residency. Include a peer comparison ranking him among the top 5 percent of students I have taught.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user seeking a comprehensive MSPE-aligned evaluation

output_examples.input: Write a recommendation letter for Sarah Chen, my clinical mentor for 6 months, for internal medicine residency. Strengths: clinical skills, work ethic, teamwork.
output_examples.output:
It is with great enthusiasm that I write to recommend Sarah Chen for residency.

I have known Sarah for 6 months in my capacity as their clinical mentor.

Sarah demonstrated excellent clinical acumen and patient care skills. Sarah consistently showed exceptional dedication and reliability. Sarah worked effectively as part of the healthcare team.

Sarah ranks among the top students I have worked with during my career.

I enthusiastically recommend Sarah and would welcome them as a colleague.

output_examples.input: Write a letter for James Rodriguez, my research PI for 2 years, for surgical residency. Strengths: research, leadership, communication.
output_examples.output:
I am writing to strongly endorse James Rodriguez for surgical residency based on their research work in my laboratory.

I have known James for 2 years in my capacity as their research PI.

James produced high-quality research with strong analytical skills. James demonstrated leadership potential and initiative. James communicated clearly with colleagues and collaborators.

James ranks among the top residents I have worked with during my career.

I enthusiastically recommend James and would welcome them as a colleague.

Competencies addressed: research, leadership, communication.

best_practices: Provide specific and verifiable examples of applicant strengths to make the letter credible
Select competencies that match the requirements of the target program or position
Review and personalize the generated draft before submitting it as a final letter
anti_patterns: Using generic praise without specific examples or evidence of the applicant's abilities
Requesting letters for applicants you have not directly supervised or worked with
Including exaggerated claims about the applicant that cannot be substantiated

faq.question: What relationship types does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports three relationship types: mentor, course director, and research PI. Each has a different opening template matched to that role.

faq.question: Can I customize the enthusiasm level of the letter?
faq.answer: The skill uses an enthusiastic closing by default. You can modify the closing template in the script to use standard or strong language instead.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to ERAS or other application systems?
faq.answer: No. The skill only generates letter text. You must copy and submit the output through the appropriate application system yourself.

faq.question: What competencies can the skill address?
faq.answer: The skill includes phrases for clinical skills, work ethic, teamwork, communication, research, leadership, and professionalism. You can add more by editing the script.

faq.question: How many strengths can I include in one letter?
faq.answer: The script processes up to four strengths per request. This keeps the letter focused and prevents overly long paragraphs.

faq.question: Is the content fact-checked before output?
faq.answer: No. The skill generates text based on your input without verifying accuracy. You are responsible for reviewing and confirming all factual claims.
